text.title: Traceability Matrix Template Csv Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules every Traceability Matrix Template Csv application MUST enforce. These are HARD rules — user-supplied input cannot override them.

rule.statement: Apply Traceability Matrix Template Csv only to a single instance of 'role-business-analyst/Traceability matrix weekly refresh'. Never batch multiple unrelated decisions through one pass.
rule.rationale: Batching turns a 1-decision artefact into a debate club. Each instance gets its own record so the rationale is traceable.

rule.statement: Every input field has a declared type and source. Free-form prose without a source citation is rejected.
rule.rationale: Untyped inputs let LLMs hallucinate plausible-but-wrong values. Typed + sourced inputs make the output falsifiable.

rule.statement: The output names a single accountable owner (handle, email, or org role). No 'team' or 'we' as owner.
rule.rationale: RACI / single-throat-to-choke. Plural ownership = no ownership; future readers need a named human to chase.

rule.statement: Output carries semver-style version and last_reviewed date. Stale records (>90 days, no review) are flagged on read.
rule.rationale: Methodology outputs decay; treating them as immutable is a defect. Versioning + review-by-date forces refresh cadence.

rule.statement: Every section of the artefact cites the specific gap it closes: 'BAs need a ready-to-use matrix template + Jira export script for weekly refresh; current methodology is conceptual only.'.
rule.rationale: Without grounding, the artefact becomes generic boilerplate. The cited gap is the falsifiability anchor.

rule.statement: If the Applies-If preconditions fail OR any Skip-If condition triggers, do NOT apply this methodology. Emit a skip-record naming the failed precondition / triggered skip, and route the task to the correct methodology (or to no methodology at all).
rule.rationale: Mis-application of a methodology produces worse artefacts than no methodology at all because downstream consumers trust the schema and act on bad data. The skip-record makes the non-application traceable so future audits can detect drift.
